sentences.text: This chapter describes the scalar cryptography extension for RISC-V.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction

sentences.text: All instructions described herein use the general-purpose `X` registers, and obey the 2-read-1-write register access constraint.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction

sentences.text: These instructions are designed to be lightweight and suitable for `32` and `64` bit base architectures; from embedded IoT class cores to large, application class cores which do not implement a vector unit.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction

sentences.text: This chapter also describes the architectural interface to an Entropy Source, which can be used to generate cryptographic secrets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction

sentences.text: It also contains a mechanism allowing core implementers to provide "Constant Time Execution" guarantees in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction

sentences.text: Cryptography is a specialised subject, requiring people with many different backgrounds to cooperate in its secure and efficient implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Where possible, we have written this specification to be understandable by all, though we recognise that the motivations and references to algorithms or other specifications and standards may be unfamiliar to those who are not domain experts.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: This specification anticipates being read and acted on by various people with different backgrounds.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We have tried to capture these backgrounds here, with a brief explanation of what we expect them to know, and how it relates to the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We hope this aids people's understanding of which aspects of the specification are particularly relevant to them, and which they may (safely!) ignore or pass to a colleague.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Cryptographers and cryptographic software developers:: These are the people we expect to write code using the instructions in this specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: They should understand fairly obviously the motivations for the instructions we include, and be familiar with most of the algorithms and outside standards to which we refer.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We expect the sections on constant time execution () and the entropy source () to be chiefly understood with their help.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Computer architects:: We do not expect architects to have a cryptography background.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We nonetheless expect architects to be able to examine our instructions for implementation issues, understand how the instructions will be used in context, and advise on how best to fit the functionality the cryptographers want to the ISA interface.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Digital design engineers & micro-architects:: These are the people who will implement the specification inside a core.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Again, no cryptography expertise is assumed, but we expect them to interpret the specification and anticipate any hardware implementation issues, e.g., where high-frequency design considerations apply, or where latency/area tradeoffs exist etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: In particular, they should be aware of the literature around efficiently implementing AES and SM4 SBoxes in hardware.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Verification engineers:: Responsible for ensuring the correct implementation of the extension in hardware.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: No cryptography background is assumed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We expect them to identify interesting test cases from the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: An understanding of their real-world usage will help with this.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We do not expect verification engineers in this sense to be experts in entropy source design or certification, since this is a very specialised area.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We do expect them however to identify all of the architectural test cases around the entropy source interface.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: These are by no means the only people concerned with the specification, but they are the ones we considered most while writing it.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: RISC-V maintains a link:https://github.com/riscv/sail-riscv[formal model] of the ISA specification, implemented in the Sail ISA specification language cite:[sail].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Note that Sail refers to the specification language itself, and that there is a model of RISC-V, written using Sail.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: It is not correct to refer to "the Sail model".
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: This is ambiguous, given there are many models of different ISAs implemented using Sail.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: We refer to the Sail implementation of RISC-V as "the RISC-V Sail model".
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: The Cryptography extension uses inline Sail code snippets from the actual model to give canonical descriptions of instruction functionality.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Each instruction is accompanied by its expression in Sail, and includes calls to supporting functions which are too verbose to include directly in the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: This supporting code is listed in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: The link:https://alasdair.github.io/manual.html[Sail Manual] is recommended reading in order to best understand the code snippets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Note that this chapter contains only a subset of the formal model: refer to the formal model GitHub link:https://github.com/riscv/sail-riscv[repository] for the complete model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: In creating this proposal, we tried to adhere to the following policies:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Where there is a choice between: . supporting diverse implementation strategies for an algorithm or . supporting a single implementation style which is more performant / less expensive; the crypto extension will pick the more constrained but performant option.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: This fits a common pattern in other parts of the RISC-V specification, where recommended (but not required) instruction sequences for performing particular tasks are given as an example, such that both hardware and software implementers can optimise for only a single use-case.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: The extension will be designed to support existing standardised cryptographic constructs well.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: It will not try to support proposed standards, or cryptographic constructs which exist only in academia.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Cryptographic standards which are settled upon concurrently with or after the RISC-V cryptographic extension standardisation will be dealt with by future additions to, or versions of, the RISC-V cryptographic standard extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: It is anticipated that the NIST Lightweight Cryptography contest and the NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography contest may be dealt with this way, depending on timescales.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Historically, there has been some discussion cite:[LSYRR:04] on how newly supported operations in general-purpose computing might enable new bases for cryptographic algorithms.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: The standard will not try to anticipate new useful low-level operations which may be useful as building blocks for future cryptographic constructs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Regarding side-channel countermeasures: Where relevant, proposed instructions must aim to remove the possibility of any timing side-channels.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: For side-channels based on power or electro-magnetic (EM) measurements, the extension will not aim to support countermeasures which are implemented above the ISA abstraction layer.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Recommendations will be given where relevant on how micro-architectures can implement instructions in a power/EM side-channel resistant way.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: The group of extensions introduced by the Scalar Cryptography Instruction Set Extension is listed here.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: Detection of individual cryptography extensions uses the unified software-based RISC-V discovery method.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: .A note on extension rationale [NOTE, caption="SH"] Specialist encryption and decryption instructions are separated into different functional groups because some use cases (e.g., Galois/Counter Mode in TLS 1.3) do not require decryption functionality.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: The NIST and ShangMi algorithms suites are separated because their usefulness is heavily dependent on the countries a device is expected to operate in. NIST ciphers are a part of most standardised internet protocols, while ShangMi ciphers are required for use in China.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: [insns-aes32dsi, reftext="AES final round decrypt (RV32)"]
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions

sentences.text: Synopsis:: AES final round decryption instruction for RV32.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: Mnemonic:: aes32dsi rd, rs1, rs2, bs
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: Encoding:: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ {bits: 7, name: 0x33}, {bits: 5, name: 'rd'}, {bits: 3, name: 0x0}, {bits: 5, name: 'rs1'}, {bits: 5, name: 'rs2'}, {bits: 5, name: 0x15}, {bits: 2, name: 'bs'}, ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: Description:: This instruction sources a single byte from `rs2` according to `bs`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: To this it applies the inverse AES SBox operation, and XOR's the result with `rs1`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (AES32DSI (bs,rs2,rs1,rd)) = { let shamt : bits( 5) = bs @ 0b000; let si : bits( 8) = (X(rs2)[31..0] >> shamt)[7..0]; let so : bits(32) = 0x000000 @ aessboxinv(si); let result : bits(32) = X(rs1)[31..0] ^ rol32(so, unsigned(shamt)); X(rd) = EXTS(result); RETIRESUCCESS } --
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes32dsi

sentences.text: Synopsis:: This instruction accelerates the inverse MixColumns step of the AES Block Cipher, and is used to aid creation of the decryption KeySchedule.

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes64ks1i

sentences.text: Description:: This instruction implements the rotation, SubBytes and Round Constant addition steps of the AES block cipher Key Schedule.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes64ks1i

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes64ks1i

sentences.text: Note that `rnum` must be in the range `0x0..0xA`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes64ks1i

sentences.text: The values `0xB..0xF` are reserved.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > aes64ks1i

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (AES64KS1I(rnum, rs1, rd)) = { if(unsigned(rnum) > 10) then { handleillegal(); RETIRESUCCESS } else { let tmp1 : bits(32) = X(rs1)[63..32]; let rc : bits(32) = aesdecodercon(rnum); let tmp2 : bits(32) = if (rnum 0xA) then tmp1 else ror32(tmp1, 8); let tmp3 : bits(32) = aessubwordfwd(tmp2); let result : bits(64) = (tmp3 ^ rc) @ (tmp3 ^ rc); X(rd) = EXTZ(result); RETIRESUCCESS } } --

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > rorw

sentences.text: Synopsis:: Implements the Sigma0 transformation function as used in the SHA2-256 hash function cite:[nist:fips:180:4].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: Mnemonic:: sha256sig0 rd, rs1
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: Encoding:: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ {bits: 7, name: 0x13}, {bits: 5, name: 'rd'}, {bits: 3, name: 0x1}, {bits: 5, name: 'rs1'}, {bits: 5, name: 0x2}, {bits: 5, name: 0x8}, {bits: 2, name: 0x0}, ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: Description:: This instruction is supported for both RV32 and RV64 base architectures.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: For RV32, the entire `XLEN` source register is operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: For RV64, the low `32` bits of the source register are operated on, and the result sign extended to `XLEN` bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: Though named for SHA2-256, the instruction works for both the SHA2-224 and SHA2-256 parameterizations as described in cite:[nist:fips:180:4].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha256sig0

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (SHA256SIG0(rs1,rd)) = { let inb : bits(32) = X(rs1)[31..0]; let result : bits(32) = ror32(inb, 7) ^ ror32(inb, 18) ^ (inb >> 3); X(rd) = EXTS(result); RETIRESUCCESS } --

sentences.text: Synopsis:: Implements the high half of the Sigma0 transformation, as used in the SHA2-512 hash function cite:[nist:fips:180:4].

sentences.text: Description:: This instruction is implemented on RV32 only.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: Used to compute the Sigma0 transform of the SHA2-512 hash function in conjunction with the instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: The transform is a 64-bit to 64-bit function, so the input and output are each represented by two 32-bit registers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: [NOTE] .Note to software developers The entire Sigma0 transform for SHA2-512 may be computed on RV32 using the following instruction sequence:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: sha512sig0l t0, a0, a1 sha512sig0h t1, a1, a0
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sha512sig0h

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (SHA512SIG0H(rs2, rs1, rd)) = { X(rd) = EXTS((X(rs1) >> 1) ^ (X(rs1) >> 7) ^ (X(rs1) >> 8) ^ (X(rs2) << 31) ^ (X(rs2) << 24) ); RETIRESUCCESS } --

sentences.text: Synopsis:: Accelerates the block encrypt/decrypt operation of the SM4 block cipher cite:[gbt:sm4, iso:sm4].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: Mnemonic:: sm4ed rd, rs1, rs2, bs
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: Encoding:: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ {bits: 7, name: 0x33}, {bits: 5, name: 'rd'}, {bits: 3, name: 0x0}, {bits: 5, name: 'rs1'}, {bits: 5, name: 'rs2'}, {bits: 5, name: 0x18}, {bits: 2, name: 'bs'}, ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: Description:: Implements a T-tables in hardware style approach to accelerating the SM4 round function. A byte is extracted from `rs2` based on `bs`, to which the SBox and linear layer transforms are applied, before the result is XOR'd with `rs1` and written back to `rd`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: This instruction exists on RV32 and RV64 base architectures.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: On RV64, the 32-bit result is sign extended to XLEN bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instructions > sm4ed

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (SM4ED (bs,rs2,rs1,rd)) = { let shamt : bits(5) = bs @ 0b000; let sbin : bits(8) = (X(rs2)[31..0] >> shamt)[7..0]; let x : bits(32) = 0x000000 @ sm4sbox(sbin); let y : bits(32) = x ^ (x << 8) ^ ( x << 2) ^ (x << 18) ^ ((x & 0x0000003F) << 26) ^ ((x & 0x000000C0) << 10); let z : bits(32) = rol32(y, unsigned(shamt)); let result: bits(32) = z ^ X(rs1)[31..0]; X(rd) = EXTS(result); RETIRESUCCESS } --

sentences.text: The `seed` CSR provides an interface to a NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] or BSI AIS-31 cite:[KiSc11] compliant physical Entropy Source (ES).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

sentences.text: An entropy source, by itself, is not a cryptographically secure Random Bit Generator (RBG), but can be used to build standard (and nonstandard) RBGs of many types with the help of symmetric cryptography.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

sentences.text: Expected usage is to condition (typically with SHA-2/3) the output from an entropy source and use it to seed a cryptographically secure Deterministic Random Bit Generator (DRBG) such as AES-based `CTRDRBG` cite:[BaKe15].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

sentences.text: The combination of an Entropy Source, Conditioning, and a DRBG can be used to create random bits securely cite:[BaKeMc:25].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

sentences.text: See for a non-normative description of a certification and self-certification procedures, design rationale, and more detailed suggestions on how the entropy source output can be used.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

sentences.text: `seed` is an unprivileged CSR located at address `0x015`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The 32-bit contents of `seed` are as follows:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: | |Bits |Name |Description
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR
Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: |`29:24` |reserved |For future use by the RISC-V specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: |`23:16` |custom |Designated for custom and experimental use.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: |`15: 0` |`entropy` |16 bits of randomness, only when `OPST=ES16`. |
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Attempts to access the `seed` CSR using a read-only CSR-access instruction (`CSRRS`/`CSRRC` with rs1=`x0` or `CSRRSI`/`CSRRCI` with uimm=0) raise an illegal-instruction exception; any other CSR-access instruction may be used to access `seed`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The write value (in `rs1` or `uimm`) must be ignored by implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The purpose of the write is to signal polling and flushing.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Software normally uses the instruction `csrrw rd, seed, x0` to read the `seed` CSR.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Encoding:: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ {bits: 7, name: 0x73, attr: "SYSTEM"}, {bits: 5, name: 'rd'}, {bits: 3, name: 0x1, attr: "CSRRW"}, {bits: 5, name: 0x0, attr: "x0"}, {bits: 12, name: 0x15, attr: "seed = 0x015"}, ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The `seed` CSR is also access controlled by execution mode, and attempted read or write access will raise an illegal-instruction exception outside M mode unless access is explicitly granted.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The status bits `seed[31:30]` = `OPST` may be `ES16` (10), indicating successful polling, or one of three entropy polling failure statuses `BIST` (00), `WAIT` (01), or `DEAD` (11), discussed below.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Each returned `seed[15:0]` = `entropy` value represents unique randomness when `OPST`=`ES16` (`seed[31:30]` = `10`), even if its numerical value is the same as that of a previously polled `entropy` value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The implementation requirements of `entropy` bits are defined in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: When `OPST` is not `ES16`, `entropy` must be set to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: An implementation may safely set reserved and custom bits to zeros.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: For security reasons, the interface guarantees that secret `entropy` words are not made available multiple times.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Hence polling (reading) must also have the side effect of clearing (wipe-on-read) the `entropy` contents and changing the state to `WAIT` (unless there is `entropy` immediately available for `ES16`).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Other states (`BIST`, `WAIT`, and `DEAD`) may be unaffected by polling.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The Status Bits returned in `seed[31:30]`=`OPST`:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: `00` - `BIST` indicates that Built-In Self-Test "on-demand" (BIST) testing is being performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: If `OPST` returns temporarily to `BIST` from any other state, this signals a non-fatal self-test alarm, which is non-actionable, apart from being logged.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: Such a `BIST` alarm must be latched until polled at least once to enable software to record its occurrence.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: `01` - `WAIT` means that a sufficient amount of entropy is not yet available.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: This is not an error condition and may (in fact) be more frequent than ES16 since physical entropy sources often have low bandwidth.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: `10` - `ES16` indicates success; the low bits `seed[15:0]` will have 16 bits of randomness (`entropy`), which is guaranteed to meet certain minimum entropy requirements, regardless of implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: `11` - `DEAD` is an unrecoverable self-test error.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: This may indicate a hardware fault, a security issue, or (extremely rarely) a type-1 statistical false positive in the continuous testing procedures.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: In case of a fatal failure, an immediate lockdown may also be an appropriate response in dedicated security devices.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: **Example.** `0x8000ABCD` is a valid `ES16` status output, with `0xABCD` being the `entropy` value. `0xFFFFFFFF` is an invalid output (`DEAD`) with no `entropy` value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: image::esstate.svg[title="Entropy Source state transition diagram.", align="center",scaledwidth=40%] Normally the operational state alternates between WAIT (no data) and ES16, which means that 16 bits of randomness (`entropy`) have been polled. BIST (Built-in Self-Test) only occurs after reset or to signal a non-fatal self-test alarm (if reached after WAIT or ES16). DEAD is an unrecoverable error state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

sentences.text: The output `entropy` (`seed[15:0]` in ES16 state) is not necessarily fully conditioned randomness due to hardware and energy limitations of smaller, low-powered implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: However, minimum requirements are defined.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: The main requirement is that 2-to-1 cryptographic post-processing in 256-bit input blocks will yield 128-bit "full entropy" output blocks.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: Entropy source users may make this conservative assumption but are not prohibited from using more than twice the number of seed bits relative to the desired resulting entropy.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: An implementation of the entropy source should meet at least one of the following requirements sets in order to be considered a secure and safe design:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: : A physical entropy source meeting NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] criteria with evaluated min-entropy of 192 bits for each 256 output bits (min-entropy rate 0.75).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: : A physical entropy source meeting the AIS-31 PTG.2 cite:[KiSc11] criteria, implying average Shannon entropy rate 0.997.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: The source must also meet the NIST 800-90B min-entropy rate 192/256 = 0.75.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: : A virtual entropy source is a DRBG seeded from a physical entropy source.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: It must have at least a 256-bit (Post-Quantum Category 5) internal security level.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: All implementations must signal initialization, test mode, and health alarms as required by respective standards.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: This may require the implementer to add non-standard (custom) test interfaces in a secure and safe manner, an example of which is described in
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

sentences.text: All NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] required components and health test mechanisms must be implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: The entropy requirement is satisfied if 128 bits of full entropy can be obtained from each 256-bit (16*16 -bit) successful, but possibly non-consecutive `entropy` (ES16) output sequence using a vetted conditioning algorithm such as a cryptographic hash (See Section 3.1.5.1.1, SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18]).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: In practice, a min-entropy rate of 0.75 or larger is required for this.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: Note that 128 bits of estimated input min-entropy does not yield 128 bits of conditioned, full entropy in SP 800-90B/C evaluation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: Instead, the implication is that every 256-bit sequence should have min-entropy of at least 128+64 = 192 bits, as discussed in SP 800-90C cite:[BaKeMc:25]; the likelihood of successfully "guessing" an individual 256-bit output sequence should not be higher than 2^-192^ even with (almost) unconstrained amount of entropy source data and computational power.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: Rather than attempting to define all the mathematical and architectural properties that the entropy source must satisfy, we define that the physical entropy source be strong and robust enough to pass the equivalent of NIST SP 800-90 evaluation and certification for full entropy when conditioned cryptographically in ratio 2:1 with 128-bit output blocks.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: Even though the requirement is defined in terms of 128-bit full entropy blocks, we recommend 256-bit security.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: This can be accomplished by using at least 512 `entropy` bits to initialize a DRBG that has 256-bit security.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

sentences.text: For alternative Common Criteria certification (or self-certification), AIS 31 PTG.2 class cite:[KiSc11] (Sect. 4.3.) required hardware components and mechanisms must be implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

sentences.text: In addition to AIS-31 PTG.2 randomness requirements (Shannon entropy rate of 0.997 as evaluated in that standard), the overall min-entropy requirement of remains, as discussed in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

sentences.text: Note that 800-90B min-entropy can be significantly lower than AIS-31 Shannon entropy.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

sentences.text: These two metrics should not be equated or confused with each other.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

sentences.text: NOTE: A virtual source is not an ISA compliance requirement.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: It is defined for the benefit of the RISC-V security ecosystem so that virtual systems may have a consistent level of security.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: A virtual source is not a physical entropy source but provides additional protection against covert channels, depletion attacks, and host identification in operating environments that can not be entirely trusted with direct access to a hardware resource.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: Despite limited trust, implementers should try to guarantee that even such environments have sufficient entropy available for secure cryptographic operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: A virtual source traps access to the `seed` CSR, emulates it, or otherwise implements it, possibly without direct access to a physical entropy source.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: The output can be cryptographically secure pseudorandomness instead of real entropy, but must have at least 256-bit security, as defined below. A virtual source is intended especially for guest operating systems, sandboxes, emulators, and similar use cases.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: As a technical definition, a random-distinguishing attack against the output should require computational resources comparable or greater than those required for exhaustive key search on a secure block cipher with a 256-bit key (e.g., AES 256).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: This applies to both classical and quantum computing models, but only classical information flows.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: The virtual source security requirement maps to Post-Quantum Security Category 5 cite:[NI16].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: Any implementation of the `seed` CSR that limits the security strength shall not reduce it to less than 256 bits.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: If the security level is under 256 bits, then the interface must not be available.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: A virtual entropy source does not need to implement `WAIT` or `BIST` states.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: It should fail (`DEAD`) if the host DRBG or entropy source fails and there is insufficient seeding material for the host DRBG.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

sentences.text: The Zkr extension adds the `SSEED` and `USEED` fields to the `mseccfg` CSR to control access to the `seed` CSR from U, S, or HS modes (see Privileged ISA specification).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: Systems should implement carefully considered access control policies from lower privilege modes to physical entropy sources.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: The system can trap attempted access to `seed` and feed a less privileged client virtual entropy source data () instead of invoking an SP 800-90B () or PTG.2 () physical entropy source.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: Emulated `seed` data generation is made with an appropriately seeded, secure software DRBG.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: See for security considerations related to direct access to entropy sources.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: Implementations may implement `mseccfg` such that `[s,u]seed` is a read-only constant value `0`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: Software may discover if access to the `seed` CSR can be enabled in U and S mode by writing a `1` to `[s,u]seed` and reading back the result.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

sentences.text: The Zkt extension attests that the machine has data-independent execution time for a safe subset of instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt

sentences.text: This property is commonly called "constant-time" although should not be taken with that literal meaning.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt

sentences.text: All currently defined cryptographic instructions (Zk* and Zbk* extensions) are on this list, together with a set of relevant supporting instructions from I, M, C, and B extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt

sentences.text: .Note to software developers [NOTE,caption="SH"] Failure to prevent leakage of sensitive parameters via the direct timing channel is considered a serious security vulnerability and will typically result in a CERT CVE security advisory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt

sentences.text: An "ISA contract" is made between a programmer and the RISC-V implementation that Zkt instructions do not leak information about processed secret data (plaintext, keying information, or other "sensitive security parameters" -- FIPS 140-3 term) through differences in execution latency.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Zkt does not define a set of instructions available in the core; it just restricts the behaviour of certain instructions if those are implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Currently, the scope of this chapter is within scalar RV32/RV64 processors.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Vector cryptography instructions (and appropriate vector support instructions) will be added later, as will other security-related functions that wish to assert leakage-free execution latency properties.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Loads, stores, conditional branches are excluded, along with a set of instructions that are rarely necessary to process secret data.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Also excluded are instructions for which workarounds exist in standard cryptographic middleware due to the limitations of other ISA processors.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: The stated goal is that OpenSSL, BoringSSL (Android), the Linux Kernel, and similar trusted software will not have directly observable timing side channels when compiled and running on a Zkt-enabled RISC-V target.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: The Zkt extension explicitly states many of the common latency assumptions made by cryptography developers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Vendors do not have to implement all of the list's instructions to be Zkt compliant; however, if they claim to have Zkt and implement any of the listed instructions, it must have data-independent latency.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: For example, many simple RV32I and RV64I cores (without Multiply, Compressed, Bitmanip, or Cryptographic extensions) are technically compliant with Zkt. A constant-time AES can be implemented on them using "bit-slice" techniques, but it will be excruciatingly slow when compared to implementation with AES instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: There are no guarantees that even a bit-sliced cipher implementation (largely based on boolean logic instructions) is secure on a core without Zkt attestation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Out-of-order implementations adhering to Zkt are still free to fuse, crack, change or even ignore sequences of instructions, so long as the optimisations are applied deterministically, and not based on operand data.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: The guiding principle should be that no information about the data being operated on should be leaked based on the execution latency.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: .Note to software developers [WARNING,caption="SH"] Programming techniques can only mitigate leakage directly caused by arithmetic, caches, and branches.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Other ISAs have had micro-architectural issues such as Spectre, Meltdown, Speculative Store Bypass, Rogue System Register Read, Lazy FP State Restore, Bounds Check Bypass Store, TLBleed, and L1TF/Foreshadow, etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: See e.g. link:https://github.com/nsacyber/Hardware-and-Firmware-Security-Guidance[NSA Hardware and Firmware Security Guidance]
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: It is not within the remit of this proposal to mitigate these micro-architectural leakages.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

sentences.text: Timing attacks are much more powerful than was realised before the 2010s, which has led to a significant mitigation effort in current cryptographic code-bases. * Cryptography developers use static and dynamic security testing tools to trace the handling of secret information and detect occasions where it influences a branch or is used for a table lookup. * Architectural testing for Zkt can be pragmatic and semi-formal; security by design against basic timing attacks can usually be achieved via conscious implementation (of relevant iterative multi-cycle instructions or instructions composed of micro-ops) in way that avoids data-dependent latency. * Laboratory testing may utilize statistical timing attack leakage analysis techniques such as those described in ISO/IEC 17825 cite:[IS16]. * Binary executables should not contain secrets in the instruction encodings (Kerckhoffs's principle), so instruction timing may leak information about immediates, ordering of input registers, etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: There may be an exception to this in systems where a binary loader modifies the executable for purposes of relocation -- and it is desirable to keep the execution location (PC) secret.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: This is why instructions such as LUI, AUIPC, and ADDI are on the list. * The rules used by audit tools are relatively simple to understand.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: Very briefly; we call the plaintext, secret keys, expanded keys, nonces, and other such variables "secrets". A secret variable (arithmetically) modifying any other variable/register turns that into a secret too.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: If a secret ends up in address calculation affecting a load or store, that is a violation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: If a secret affects a branch's condition, that is also a violation. A secret variable location or register becomes a non-secret via specific zeroization/sanitisation or by being declared ciphertext (or otherwise no-longer-secret information).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: In essence, secrets can only "touch" instructions on the Zkt list while they are secrets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

sentences.text: HINT instruction forms (typically encodings with rd=`x0`) are excluded from the data-independent time requirement. * Floating point (F, D, Q, L extensions) are currently excluded from the constant-time requirement as they have very few applications in standardised cryptography.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Specific Instruction Rationale

sentences.text: We may consider adding floating point add, sub, multiply as a constant time requirement for some floating point extension in case a specific algorithm (such as the PQC Signature algorithm Falcon) becomes critical. * Cryptographers typically assume division to be variable-time (while multiplication is constant time) and implement their Montgomery reduction routines with that assumption. * Zicsr, Zifencei are excluded. * Some instructions are on the list simply because we see no harm in including them in testing scope.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Specific Instruction Rationale
